senses.definition: Obsolete word for people who sell poultry and game birds as food.
examples: In the old market records, several poulters are listed by name.
City poulters supplied chickens and geese to the royal kitchens.

senses.definition: A soft, usually warm, wet mass spread on cloth and put on the skin to reduce pain or swelling.
examples: The nurse put a warm poultice on his injured knee to reduce the swelling.
Her grandmother used a herbal poultice to treat insect bites.

senses.definition: Covered or treated with a poultice.
examples: The doctor poulticed the wound with a thick paste of herbs.
Her sprained ankle was carefully poulticed and bandaged.

senses.definition: More than one poultice.
examples: Traditional healers used various poultices made from local plants.
The clinic prepared fresh poultices for the patients each morning.

senses.definition: In the manner of a poultice or by using a poultice.
examples: The crushed leaves were applied poulticewise to the burn.
The clay was spread poulticewise over the sore skin.

senses.definition: Putting a poultice on a part of the body as a treatment.
examples: They were poulticing his shoulder to ease the pain.
She spent the evening poulticing her swollen foot.

senses.definition: Rare plural form of poultry, referring to different kinds or groups of domesticated birds raised for meat or eggs.
examples: The farm displayed various poultries from around the region.
Different poultries require different feeding schedules.

senses.definition: Very rare spelling of poltroon, meaning a person who is extremely cowardly.
examples: The knight called his enemy a miserable poultroone.
Only a poultroone would flee at the first sign of danger.

senses.definition: Plural of poultroone, an archaic word for very cowardly people.
examples: He dismissed them all as useless poultroones.
Stories mocked the poultroones who ran from every battle.

senses.definition: Chickens, turkeys, ducks, and other birds kept for their meat or eggs.
examples: They raise poultry and vegetables on their small farm.
The market sells fresh poultry every morning.

senses.definition: Meat from birds such as chicken or turkey, used as food.
examples: The restaurant serves a variety of poultry dishes.
She does not eat red meat but she does eat poultry.

senses.definition: The world of poultry farming and trade, or all poultry collectively, used in a humorous or informal way.
examples: He is a well‑known figure in the world of poultrydom.
The new regulations shocked poultrydom across the country.

senses.definition: A person who breeds, raises, or studies poultry.
examples: As a dedicated poultryist, she attends many agricultural fairs.
The magazine is written mainly for poultryists and farmers.

senses.definition: Having no poultry, or containing no poultry meat.
examples: Due to the disease outbreak, many farms were suddenly poultryless.
The menu offers several poultryless options for vegetarians.

senses.definition: Resembling poultry in some way, especially in appearance or taste.
examples: The meat had a poultrylike flavor.
They discovered a large, poultrylike bird on the island.

senses.definition: A person, usually a man, who raises or sells poultry.
examples: The poultryman showed us different breeds of chickens.
As a young poultryman, he started with only a few hens.

senses.definition: Plural of poultryman, meaning men who raise or sell poultry.
examples: Local poultrymen met to discuss new farming regulations.
The association of poultrymen holds a fair every year.

senses.definition: Designed so that poultry cannot get through, escape, or damage something.
examples: They built a poultryproof fence around the garden.
The shed door is not yet poultryproof.

senses.definition: Young birds, especially young turkeys, that are being raised for meat.
examples: The farmer keeps the poults in a warm shed.
Turkey poults require special feed in their first weeks.

senses.definition: A type of hard green stone, similar to jade, traditionally valued and carved by Māori people in New Zealand.
examples: He wore a pendant carved from pounamu.
Pounamu is highly treasured in Māori culture.

senses.definition: To jump or move suddenly in order to catch or attack someone or something.
examples: The cat hid behind the sofa, ready to pounce on the toy.
The lion waited silently, then pounced on its prey.

senses.definition: To react very quickly to use an opportunity or to criticize a mistake.
examples: Investors were quick to pounce on the chance to buy cheap shares.
The media pounced on the politician's careless remark.

senses.definition: A sudden jump or attack in order to catch something.
examples: With one swift pounce, the cat caught the mouse.
The bird made a sharp pounce from the tree.

senses.definition: Jumped or moved suddenly to catch or attack something or someone.
examples: The tiger pounced without giving its victim a chance to escape.
She pounced on the falling book before it hit the floor.

senses.definition: Something or someone that pounces.
examples: The cat is a great pouncer when it plays with string.
As a bargain pouncer, she watches online prices every day.

senses.definition: People or animals that pounce, especially quickly using chances or weaknesses.
examples: Online pouncers bought all the tickets within minutes.
The kittens are energetic little pouncers.

senses.definition: Jumps or moves suddenly to catch or attack someone or something.
examples: The cat often pounces on my feet under the table.
He watches carefully and pounces whenever there is a mistake.

senses.definition: An old term for a small container or ball for perfume, often carried or worn.
examples: She carried a silver pouncet at her waist.
The nobleman raised his pouncet to his nose as he entered the hall.

senses.definition: Plural of pouncet, meaning several small perfume containers or balls.
examples: The jeweler displayed a set of gold pouncets.
Ladies of the court wore delicate pouncets around their necks.

senses.definition: Very rare form probably intended as punching or pouching; not used in standard contemporary English with a fixed meaning.
examples: The term "pounching" does not appear in standard modern dictionaries.
Writers today almost never use the word "pounching".

senses.definition: Jumping or moving suddenly to catch or attack someone or something.
examples: The cat spent the afternoon pouncing on leaves in the garden.
He waited in silence, pouncing when his opponent relaxed.

senses.definition: In a sudden, attacking way, like something that pounces.
examples: The cat moved pouncingly toward the toy.
He spoke pouncingly, ready to pick up on any mistake.

senses.definition: Liking to pounce, or tending to pounce in a sudden or playful way.
examples: Their kitten is very pouncy and full of energy.
The children were in a pouncy mood, jumping on the sofa.

senses.definition: A unit of weight equal to about 454 grams, used especially in the UK and US.
examples: He lost five pounds in a month.
The baby weighed eight pounds at birth.

senses.definition: The basic unit of money in the UK and some other countries.
examples: This book costs ten pounds.
She changed her dollars into pounds at the airport.

senses.definition: A place where stray dogs, cats, or other animals are kept until they are collected or rehomed.
examples: They adopted their dog from the local pound.
Lost animals are taken to the city pound.

senses.definition: To hit something or someone hard again and again.
examples: The waves pounded the rocks all night.
He pounded the table to show his anger.

senses.definition: If your heart or head pounds, it beats or feels strongly and quickly.
examples: Her heart was pounding with fear.
After running up the stairs, his head was pounding.

senses.definition: An amount of money charged or paid that is calculated by the pound in weight or value.
examples: The warehouse charges a small poundage on stored goods.
A poundage fee is added to large cash deposits.

senses.definition: The total weight in pounds of something.
examples: The total poundage of the shipment must be declared.
They reduced the poundage of luggage allowed on flights.

senses.definition: Plural of poundage, especially different fees or weights measured in pounds.
examples: Different poundages apply to different types of transaction.
The report lists the poundages of all the consignments.

senses.definition: An old unit of force in the foot‑pound‑second system, equal to the force needed to accelerate a one‑pound mass by one foot per second each second.
examples: The poundal is rarely used in modern physics.
Older textbooks sometimes give values in poundals.

senses.definition: Plural of poundal, referring to more than one unit of this old measure of force.
examples: The table lists several forces in poundals.
Engineers no longer express calculations in poundals.

senses.definition: An old legal term for the act of breaking open a pound to free animals kept there.
examples: He was fined heavily for poundbreach after releasing the seized cattle.
Poundbreach was once treated as a serious offence.

senses.definition: A rich, heavy cake traditionally made with equal weights of flour, butter, sugar, and eggs.
examples: She served slices of lemon poundcake with tea.
Homemade poundcake is dense but very moist.

senses.definition: More than one poundcake.
examples: The bakery displayed several poundcakes in the window.
She baked two poundcakes for the party.

senses.definition: Hit something hard again and again, or crushed it by hitting repeatedly.
examples: The rain pounded the roof all night.
She pounded the spices with a heavy mortar.

senses.definition: Describes a heart or head that beat or throbbed strongly and quickly.
examples: Her heart pounded as she waited for the results.
His head pounded after the loud concert.

senses.definition: Something that weighs a stated number of pounds, especially a gun or hammer.
examples: The soldiers fired a six‑pounder at the enemy fort.
He bought a three‑pounder hammer for the job.

senses.definition: A tool or person that pounds or crushes things.
examples: Use a meat pounder to flatten the cutlets.
He is a real pavement pounder, walking for hours every day.

senses.definition: More than one pounder, either tools, guns, or people that pound.
examples: The artillery unit operated several large pounders.
The kitchen drawers are full of different pounders and grinders.

senses.definition: Repeated heavy hitting or striking.
examples: We could hear the pounding of the drums from across the field.
The pounding on the door woke the whole family.

senses.definition: A very bad defeat.
examples: The team took a real pounding in yesterday's game.
Their shares have taken a pounding on the stock market.

senses.definition: Describing a strong, repeated beating feeling or sound.
examples: He felt a pounding headache after the long journey.
They marched to the sound of pounding drums.

senses.definition: Repeated occasions of heavy hitting, loud beats, or severe defeats.
examples: The walls shook with the poundings of the machinery.
After several poundings, the team finally won a match.

senses.definition: An official in charge of a pound where stray or seized animals are kept.
examples: The poundkeeper recorded the arrival of each stray cow.
Complaints about the poundkeeper increased after several animals went missing.

senses.definition: Having no pounds, especially no money in pounds or no weight measured in pounds.
examples: After paying the bill, he felt completely poundless.
In a poundless system, all weights are given in kilograms.

senses.definition: Similar to a pound in form, function, or value.
examples: They introduced a poundlike unit of currency.
The shelter built a poundlike enclosure behind the clinic.

senses.definition: An old term for a man responsible for a pound or for impounding animals.
examples: The poundman collected fees from those reclaiming their animals.
Villagers brought stray cattle to the poundman.

senses.definition: A person in charge of an animal pound, especially in older legal or local systems.
examples: The poundmaster kept careful records of every animal brought in.
Farmers had to pay the poundmaster before taking their cattle home.

senses.definition: Very rare term probably referring to meal or grain sold or measured by the pound.
examples: In old records, customers paid for poundmeal at a fixed rate.
The miller listed several sacks of poundmeal in his accounts.

senses.definition: Plural of pound, used for weight units.
examples: The suitcases together weigh more than thirty pounds.
She lost several pounds after starting her new diet.

senses.definition: Plural of pound, used for money units.
examples: They spent hundreds of pounds on repairs.
The ticket cost twenty pounds.

senses.definition: Hits something hard again and again, or causes a strong, repeated beating.
examples: He pounds on the door when he forgets his keys.
Her heart pounds whenever she speaks in public.
